rule: Primary text must strongly contrast against the actual card surface, not only the page background.
why: Most dashboard reading happens inside translucent tiles.

rule: Secondary text must remain readable on a phone without squinting; do not use decorative gray for operational facts.
why: Project paths, TODOs, and notes carry real state.

rule: Status pills need both color and enough text contrast; color alone is not the information.
why: Phone screens and tired eyes make low-saturation badges fail.

rule: Use one expressive readable family, but keep body text at normal weights and avoid ultra-light text for facts.
why: Thin text looks premium on desktop and weak on mobile.

rule: Use at least 1.3 line-height for dense card text and 1.4 or higher for paragraphs.
Clamp long descriptions and expose detail on tap instead of shrinking text below readable size.
Favor tiles with strong title, compact facts, and a short memory section over row-heavy layouts.
why: Tiles fit the dashboard philosophy and scan faster.

rule: Avoid horizontal scroll at phone width; use one-column cards or focused overlays.
why: The phone is the primary review surface.

rule: Use borders, shadows, and surface opacity to separate cards before adding more text.
why: Visual grouping should reduce cognitive load.

rule: When changing visuals, update theme tokens, affected components, and contrast tests together.
why: Prevents one-off fixes that regress later.

rule: Write durable UI preferences into TSV registries, not Markdown notes.
why: Keeps future LLM turns fast and machine-readable.

rule: After visual edits, run the full check gate and restart the local dashboard service.
why: Phone review should always see verified output.
